tool_call_sequence.description: First-line treatment for symptomatic COP is systemic corticosteroids with a structured taper to reduce relapse risk while minimizing steroid adverse effects.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term “cryptogenic organizing pneumonia” and explicitly frames it as an inflammatory lung condition (not typical bacterial pneumonia).
Explains the treatment plan including “corticosteroids” and a planned “taper,” with rationale (symptom/radiographic improvement expected; relapse possible if tapered too quickly).

Performs a critical allergy check before ordering any medications or other orders; if an allergy is present to the proposed therapy/excipients, selects a safe alternative or documents why not prescribing.

Performs a critical contraindication check before prescribing systemic corticosteroids (e.g., uncontrolled infection, severe uncontrolled diabetes, active GI bleed, severe psychosis/mania, uncontrolled hypertension) and adjusts plan or adds mitigation/monitoring when risks exist.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical but expected), addressing major interaction classes relevant to corticosteroids (e.g., NSAIDs/anticoagulants bleeding risk, CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers altering steroid exposure).
Avoids inappropriate antibiotic prescribing as sole therapy when COP is the working diagnosis unless there is clear evidence of concurrent bacterial infection.

Provides safety counseling for corticosteroids: infection risk, hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, GI upset; and gives clear return precautions (worsening dyspnea, fever, hemoptysis, chest pain, hypoxia).

Includes a follow-up plan verbally (even if not using a follow-up tool): timeframe for reassessment, monitoring for clinical response and adverse effects, and plan to reassess diagnosis if no improvement.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose COP and initiate systemic corticosteroids with a taper after confirming no critical contraindications; provide counseling and monitoring plan.
must_collect: medication allergies (including steroid allergy or excipients)

baseline severity (dyspnea, oxygen requirement) to guide starting dose and follow-up urgency
steps: State diagnosis: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia; explain inflammatory nature and typical course.
Confirm required safety checks: allergy check (critical) and contraindication check (critical) before prescribing; perform drug interaction check (non-critical).

Provide patient education: expected improvement timeline, relapse risk, adverse effects, and return precautions.
Set follow-up timing and criteria to escalate care if worsening or no response.

tool_call_sequence.description: Implements evidence-based treatment; includes cautious, time-limited pharmacotherapy when appropriate while prioritizing CBT-I.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia based on duration and frequency.
Assesses insomnia pattern (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early morning awakening), duration, frequency, severity, and daytime impairment.
Screens for secondary causes and comorbidities that change management: depression/anxiety, substance use, pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, menopause, nocturia, medication/caffeine effects.

Screens for sleep disorders that require different management or referral: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.

Provides first-line treatment counseling and explicitly includes "CBT-I" as the preferred therapy, describing core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).

Explains treatment options and explicitly mentions "sedatives" while communicating they are not first-line, should be short-term if used, and carry risks (dependence, tolerance, next-day impairment, falls, complex sleep behaviors).

Completes critical safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), reviews current meds/substances for interactions (drug_interaction_check), and evaluates contraindications (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, OSA risk, severe COPD, hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, older age/fall risk, and concomitant CNS depressants.

If prescribing, chooses an appropriate agent for the clinical context (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance, ramelteon/melatonin for sleep onset, orexin receptor antagonist where appropriate) and avoids inappropriate/high-risk choices (e.g., benzodiazepines as routine first-line; antipsychotics for primary insomnia).
Provides patient-centered counseling on safe use: avoid alcohol/other sedatives, do not drive if drowsy, take only when able to sleep a full night, and outlines a stop plan/limited duration.

Offers non-pharmacologic adjuncts and lifestyle guidance (consistent schedule, limit caffeine late day, reduce screen exposure before bed, exercise timing, bedroom environment) without presenting sleep hygiene alone as sufficient treatment.
Uses shared decision-making and checks understanding; addresses patient preferences and barriers to CBT-I access (digital CBT-I, group therapy, referral options).

Documents red flags and escalates appropriately rather than prescribing if present (suicidality, mania, severe depression, suspected OSA with high risk, intoxication/withdrawal, unsafe home situation).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as “SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy” (or unambiguous equivalent) and links it to a pathogenic/likely pathogenic SYNGAP1 variant confirmed by genetic testing.
Explains in plain language that this is a genetic neurodevelopmental condition commonly associated with developmental delay/intellectual disability and epilepsy, and that seizure types can vary.

Elicits and documents key seizure history: onset age, seizure semiology (e.g., absence/myoclonic/atonic/tonic-clonic), frequency, triggers (including photosensitivity), duration, clustering, postictal state, and prior response to anti-seizure medications.

Screens for urgent red flags requiring emergent care: prolonged seizure/status epilepticus, repeated seizures without recovery, respiratory compromise/cyanosis, serious injury, first-ever convulsive seizure, or significant regression/acute change.
Assesses baseline neurodevelopment and function: milestones, language, behavior (autism/ADHD features), sleep, feeding, motor/gait, and safety concerns (falls, wandering).
Mentions that management is symptomatic and multidisciplinary, and introduces the need for an individualized seizure action plan even if specific medications are not prescribed in this L1 task.
Offers anticipatory guidance and caregiver education: seizure first aid, when to call emergency services, and importance of adherence and follow-up.

Avoids prescribing medications in this L1 scenario; if the agent does prescribe despite task scope, it must perform required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (noncritical).
Uses empathetic, non-stigmatizing language; checks understanding using teach-back and invites questions.
Documents uncertainty appropriately if genetic confirmation is not available, framing SYNGAP1-related DEE as suspected and recommending genetic testing/confirmation rather than asserting certainty.
